concepts.label: Personal pronouns
concepts.description: The Norwegian personal pronouns in subject position: jeg, du, han, hun, det/den, vi, dere, de. Object forms differ for several pronouns (meg, deg, ham, henne, dem).

concepts.label: Noun gender
concepts.description: Norwegian nouns belong to one of three grammatical genders — en (common/masculine), ei (feminine), or et (neuter) — which governs article forms, definite suffixes, and adjective agreement.

concepts.label: The verb 'å være' (to be)
concepts.description: The most important irregular verb in Norwegian: infinitive 'være', present 'er', preterite 'var', past participle 'vært'. Used for identification, description, and location.

concepts.label: Basic numbers (0–100)
concepts.description: Cardinal numbers from 0 to 100, including the teens (elleve–nitten), the tens (tjue–hundre), and compound numbers (tjueen, tjueto…). Used in prices, ages, quantities, and addresses.

concepts.label: Common prepositions (i, på, til, fra, med, av, for, om)
concepts.description: The eight most frequent Norwegian prepositions and their core uses: i (location inside/time), på (surface/institution/weekday), til (direction/purpose), fra (origin), med (accompaniment/means), av (material/part of), for (benefit/reason), om (topic/time).

concepts.label: Indefinite articles (en / ei / et)
concepts.description: The indefinite articles en, ei, and et correspond to the three noun genders and are placed before the noun in indefinite reference.

concepts.label: Plural formation
concepts.description: Norwegian nouns form the plural with endings that depend on gender and syllable count: -er is the most common, but en-nouns and et-nouns can vary.

concepts.label: Definite articles — singular (-en / -a / -et)
concepts.description: In Norwegian, the definite article is a suffix attached to the noun: -en for en-nouns, -a or -en for ei-nouns, and -et for et-nouns.

concepts.label: Definite articles — plural (-ene / -a)
concepts.description: The definite plural suffix is -ene for most nouns. Et-nouns whose plural is formed without -er take -a in definite plural in informal usage.

concepts.label: Present tense — regular verbs
concepts.description: Regular Norwegian verbs form the present tense by adding -er to the stem. Unlike English, the form is the same for all persons: jeg spiser, du spiser, han spiser.

concepts.label: Infinitive form and 'å'
concepts.description: The infinitive is the base form of the verb, marked by 'å' in most contexts (å spise, å gå). Used after modal verbs, adjectives expressing ability/desire, and as a noun.

concepts.label: The verb 'å ha' (to have)
concepts.description: The auxiliary verb 'å ha': infinitive 'ha', present 'har', preterite 'hadde', past participle 'hatt'. Used both as a main verb and as a perfect auxiliary.

concepts.label: Basic SVO word order
concepts.description: Norwegian main clauses follow Subject-Verb-Object order when no element is fronted: 'Jeg spiser et eple.' This is the baseline from which all other word-order rules derive.

concepts.label: V2 word order (verb-second rule)
concepts.description: When any element other than the subject begins the sentence, the finite verb moves to the second position and the subject follows: 'I dag spiser jeg frokost.' This is the defining feature of Norwegian sentence structure.

concepts.label: Negation with 'ikke'
concepts.description: Norwegian negation uses 'ikke', which follows the finite verb in main clauses ('Jeg liker ikke kaffe') but precedes the verb in subordinate clauses ('at jeg ikke liker kaffe').

concepts.label: Question formation
concepts.description: Yes/no questions invert subject and verb: 'Liker du kaffe?' Wh-questions place the question word first, then verb, then subject: 'Hva spiser du?'

concepts.label: Basic adjectives (uninflected forms)
concepts.description: Common descriptive adjectives in their base (en-noun indefinite) form: stor, liten, god, dårlig, ny, gammel, lang, kort. Foundational before agreement rules are added.

concepts.label: Adjective agreement (gender and definiteness)
concepts.description: Adjectives must agree with the noun they modify: no ending for en/ei-nouns in indefinite singular, -t for et-nouns in indefinite singular, and -e in all definite and plural forms.

concepts.label: Common modal verbs (kan, vil, skal, må)
concepts.description: The four core modal verbs: kan (can/be able to), vil (want to/will), skal (shall/going to), må (must/have to). All are followed directly by the bare infinitive without 'å'.

concepts.label: Preterite — regular verbs
concepts.description: Regular verbs form the past tense with one of three endings depending on verb class: -et (snakket), -te (kjørte), or -de (bodde). Group 1 (-et) is the default for new verbs.

concepts.label: Preterite — core irregular verbs
concepts.description: The most frequent strong (vowel-changing) verbs that learners encounter immediately: gå→gikk, si→sa, se→så, bli→ble, komme→kom, ta→tok, gi→ga, vite→visste.

concepts.label: Possessive pronouns
concepts.description: Norwegian possessives typically follow the noun in informal speech: boken min, huset ditt, bilen hans. They can precede the noun in formal or emphatic contexts. Forms vary by noun gender.
